data.title: ABN AMRO Bank (ENXTAM:ABN) Q4 Cost To Income Spike Tests Bullish Margin Narratives
data.description:
ABN AMRO Bank (ENXTAM:ABN) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €2.2 billion and basic EPS of €0.44, alongside trailing 12 month revenue of €8.7 billion and EPS of €2.71 that frame the latest print in a fuller earnings picture. Over recent quarters, the bank has seen quarterly revenue move between €2.1 billion and €2.3 billion while EPS has ranged from €0.33 to €0.99, giving investors a clear view of how profitability per share has tracked against a relatively steady top line. With net profit margins at 25.9% over the last year and cost to income ratios in the mid 60s, the key question now is how sustainable these margins look as the market reassesses ABN’s earnings power.

See our full analysis for ABN AMRO Bank.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to measure them against the widely held narratives around ABN AMRO to see which stories the latest results support and which they start to challenge.

See what the community is saying about ABN AMRO Bank

Advertisement

Margins Held While Costs Stayed Relatively High

  • Over the last 12 months ABN AMRO’s net profit margin was 25.9% with a trailing cost to income ratio of 64.4% and quarterly ratios between 59.2% and 69.7%.
  • Bulls argue that tighter cost control is a big part of the story, yet the latest figures give a mixed picture.
    • Cost to income sat at 69.7% in Q4 2025 compared with 61% in Q1 2025 and 59.2% in Q3 2024, which sits awkwardly with the bullish claim that a structurally lower cost base is already coming through.
    • At the same time, the 25.9% trailing net margin and €2.3b of trailing net income suggest profitability has held up, which still lines up with the bullish view that earnings quality is solid.

Bulls say cost discipline and digital progress are already reshaping ABN AMRO’s profitability, and these margin and cost trends show exactly where that argument is strongest and where it is still being tested. 🐂 ABN AMRO Bank Bull Case

Net Interest Metrics Versus Bear Concerns

  • Net interest margin moved in a tight band, at 1.65% in Q3 2024, 1.54% in Q1 2025, 1.49% in Q3 2025 and 1.56% in Q4 2025, while trailing 12 month NIM stood at 1.52%.
  • Bears worry that ongoing margin compression on mortgages and deposits will steadily squeeze profitability, and the current NIM pattern touches both sides of that argument.
    • The step down from 1.65% in Q3 2024 to 1.52% on a trailing basis fits the bearish point that a lower rate backdrop and competition can pressure net interest income.
    • However, the small moves between 1.49% and 1.56% across recent quarters are relatively contained, which does not yet show a steep deterioration of net interest economics in the reported numbers.

Skeptics see NIM as the weak link for ABN AMRO, and this recent range of 1.49% to 1.65% helps you judge how sharp that pressure really looks so far. 🐻 ABN AMRO Bank Bear Case

Valuation Gap Against DCF Fair Value

  • With a trailing P/E of 10.7x and a share price of €29.16, the stock is described as trading well below the DCF fair value of about €64.98.
  • Supporters of the bullish case point to this valuation gap and expected earnings growth of about 7.1% per year as key parts of their thesis.
    • The trailing P/E of 10.7x is slightly below the cited peer multiple of 11x and industry level of 11.3x, which lines up with the idea that the market prices ABN AMRO at a modest discount to other European banks.
    • The combination of that P/E discount and the wide gap to the €64.98 DCF fair value is consistent with the bullish view that current pricing does not fully reflect the bank’s reported profitability and forecast earnings growth.

data.title: Raisio (HLSE:RAIVV) Margin Improvement Reinforces Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Raisio (HLSE:RAIVV) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €55.2 million and EPS of €0.03, capping a twelve month period where trailing revenue was €224.2 million and EPS reached €0.146836, alongside year over year net earnings growth of 37.3%. Over the last six reported quarters, the company has seen quarterly revenue move between €54.5 million and €59.1 million, while quarterly EPS ranged from €0.02 to €0.05, providing a clearer picture of how the current run rate compares with recent history. With the trailing net profit margin sitting at 10.3% versus 7.5% a year earlier, the latest numbers point to a period where profitability has tightened up and margins have become a central part of the story for investors.

See our full analysis for Raisio.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to see how this margin profile and earnings run rate line up against the widely held narratives around Raisio’s growth prospects and income appeal.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

37.3% earnings growth backed by stronger margins

  • Over the last 12 months, net income reached €23.2 million and EPS was €0.146836, alongside 37.3% year over year earnings growth and a trailing net margin of 10.3% compared with 7.5% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for a more bullish take is that higher profitability metrics line up with the earnings story, as the trailing EPS of €0.146836 sits above the quarterly run rate of €0.03 to €0.05 and the 10.3% net margin is above the prior 7.5%. Together, these points suggest recent performance has been supported by both earnings and margin strength.
    • Supporters of a bullish angle can point to quarterly net income staying between €4.8 million and €7.2 million in FY 2025, versus €2.9 million to €4.9 million in the comparable FY 2024 quarters given, as evidence that the 37.3% earnings growth is backed by multiple periods rather than a single spike.
    • At the same time, the trailing revenue range of about €223.8 million to €229.1 million over the last six reported trailing twelve month points shows that the 10.3% net margin was achieved on a relatively similar revenue base. This keeps the focus on efficiency and cost control rather than just top line changes.

P/E of 18.7x versus peers and industry

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 18.7x, which sits below the cited peer average of 33.9x and slightly above the European Food industry average of 17.3x, with the current price at €2.74.
  • Skeptics looking at a more bearish angle may argue that the small premium to the broader European Food industry P/E keeps expectations in check, even though the 18.7x multiple is below the peer average. This is because forecast earnings growth of about 6.4% per year and revenue growth of roughly 3.5% per year are both described as slower than the Finnish market references.
    • Critics highlight that while the P/E discount to peers is clear on the numbers provided, the modest forecast growth rates mean the company is not being framed as a high growth outlier that might otherwise justify a much higher multiple relative to the industry average of 17.3x.
    • The combination of a 37.3% trailing earnings increase with only mid single digit forecast earnings growth can be read as a potential normalization story. This gives bears a data point when questioning how long the current earnings and valuation set up will hold.

DCF fair value gap and 5.47% dividend

  • A DCF fair value of about €3.99 per share compares with the €2.74 share price, implying roughly a 31.3% gap, while the stock also carries a 5.47% dividend yield that is described as not well covered by free cash flow.
  • What is interesting for investors balancing bullish and bearish points is that the implied discount to the €3.99 DCF fair value and the 5.47% income stream sit alongside a flag on dividend coverage. The same set of numbers both supports a valuation upside argument and gives bears a concrete cash flow based concern.
    • On the one hand, the combination of a trailing net profit of €23.2 million and the 10.3% net margin helps back the idea that the business is currently profitable enough to justify a higher value than the market price, at least according to that DCF snapshot.
    • On the other hand, the statement that the dividend is not well covered by free cash flow means income focused investors may want to look closely at how those earnings translate into cash, rather than relying only on the margin and EPS figures.

data.title: Is Pacira BioSciences, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:PCRX) Stock Price Struggling As A Result Of Its Mixed Financials?
data.description:
Pacira BioSciences (NASDAQ:PCRX) has had a rough three months with its share price down 8.6%. It seems that the market might have completely ignored the positive aspects of the company's fundamentals and decided to weigh-in more on the negative aspects. Stock prices are usually driven by a company’s financial performance over the long term, and therefore we decided to pay more attention to the company's financial performance. Particularly, we will be paying attention to Pacira BioSciences' ROE today.

Return on Equity or ROE is a test of how effectively a company is growing its value and managing investors’ money. Put another way, it reveals the company's success at turning shareholder investments into profits.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

How To Calculate Return On Equity?

The formula for return on equity is:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for Pacira BioSciences is:

2.9% = US$21m ÷ US$727m (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' is the profit over the last twelve months. That means that for every $1 worth of shareholders' equity, the company generated $0.03 in profit.

Check out our latest analysis for Pacira BioSciences

What Has ROE Got To Do With Earnings Growth?

Thus far, we have learned that ROE measures how efficiently a company is generating its profits. Depending on how much of these profits the company reinvests or "retains", and how effectively it does so, we are then able to assess a company’s earnings growth potential. Assuming all else is equal, companies that have both a higher return on equity and higher profit retention are usually the ones that have a higher growth rate when compared to companies that don't have the same features.

Pacira BioSciences' Earnings Growth And 2.9% ROE

It is quite clear that Pacira BioSciences' ROE is rather low. Not just that, even compared to the industry average of 22%, the company's ROE is entirely unremarkable. Therefore, it might not be wrong to say that the five year net income decline of 61% seen by Pacira BioSciences was possibly a result of it having a lower ROE. We reckon that there could also be other factors at play here. Such as - low earnings retention or poor allocation of capital.

So, as a next step, we compared Pacira BioSciences' performance against the industry and were disappointed to discover that while the company has been shrinking its earnings, the industry has been growing its earnings at a rate of 13% over the last few years.

Earnings growth is a huge factor in stock valuation. What investors need to determine next is if the expected earnings growth, or the lack of it, is already built into the share price. This then helps them determine if the stock is placed for a bright or bleak future. Is Pacira BioSciences fairly valued compared to other companies? These 3 valuation measures might help you decide.

Is Pacira BioSciences Efficiently Re-investing Its Profits?

Because Pacira BioSciences doesn't pay any regular dividends, we infer that it is retaining all of its profits, which is rather perplexing when you consider the fact that there is no earnings growth to show for it. It looks like there might be some other reasons to explain the lack in that respect. For example, the business could be in decline.

Summary

Overall, we have mixed feelings about Pacira BioSciences. Even though it appears to be retaining most of its profits, given the low ROE, investors may not be benefitting from all that reinvestment after all. The low earnings growth suggests our theory correct. With that said, we studied the latest analyst forecasts and found that while the company has shrunk its earnings in the past, analysts expect its earnings to grow in the future. data.title: Megacable Holdings, S. A. B. de C. V.'s (BMV:MEGACPO) Has Been On A Rise But Financial Prospects Look Weak: Is The Stock Overpriced?
data.description:
Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V (BMV:MEGACPO) has had a great run on the share market with its stock up by a significant 20% over the last three months. However, in this article, we decided to focus on its weak fundamentals, as long-term financial performance of a business is what ultimately dictates market outcomes. Particularly, we will be paying attention to Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V's ROE today.

Return on equity or ROE is an important factor to be considered by a shareholder because it tells them how effectively their capital is being reinvested. Put another way, it reveals the company's success at turning shareholder investments into profits.

How Is ROE Calculated?

The formula for return on equity is:

Return on Equity = Net Profit (from continuing operations) ÷ Shareholders' Equity

So, based on the above formula, the ROE for Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V is:

7.8% = Mex$2.7b ÷ Mex$35b (Based on the trailing twelve months to September 2025).

The 'return' refers to a company's earnings over the last year. Another way to think of that is that for every MX$1 worth of equity, the company was able to earn MX$0.08 in profit.

What Is The Relationship Between ROE And Earnings Growth?

So far, we've learned that ROE is a measure of a company's profitability. Based on how much of its profits the company chooses to reinvest or "retain", we are then able to evaluate a company's future ability to generate profits. Assuming everything else remains unchanged, the higher the ROE and profit retention, the higher the growth rate of a company compared to companies that don't necessarily bear these characteristics.

A Side By Side comparison of Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V's Earnings Growth And 7.8% ROE

It is hard to argue that Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V's ROE is much good in and of itself. An industry comparison shows that the company's ROE is not much different from the industry average of 8.6% either. Given the circumstances, the significant decline in net income by 13% seen by Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V over the last five years is not surprising.

That being said, we compared Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V's performance with the industry and were concerned when we found that while the company has shrunk its earnings, the industry has grown its earnings at a rate of 7.9% in the same 5-year period.

Earnings growth is a huge factor in stock valuation. The investor should try to establish if the expected growth or decline in earnings, whichever the case may be, is priced in. Doing so will help them establish if the stock's future looks promising or ominous. Is MEGA CPO fairly valued? This infographic on the company's intrinsic value has everything you need to know.

Is Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V Using Its Retained Earnings Effectively?

Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V has a high three-year median payout ratio of 94% (that is, it is retaining 6.3% of its profits). This suggests that the company is paying most of its profits as dividends to its shareholders. This goes some way in explaining why its earnings have been shrinking. With only a little being reinvested into the business, earnings growth would obviously be low or non-existent. You can see the 2 risks we have identified for Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V by visiting our risks dashboard for free on our platform here.

Additionally, Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V has paid dividends over a period of at least ten years, which means that the company's management is determined to pay dividends even if it means little to no earnings growth. Based on the latest analysts' estimates, we found that the company's future payout ratio over the next three years is expected to hold steady at 85%. Regardless, the future ROE for Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V is predicted to rise to 12% despite there being not much change expected in its payout ratio.

Conclusion

Overall, we would be extremely cautious before making any decision on Megacable Holdings S. A. B. de C. V. Particularly, its ROE is a huge disappointment, not to mention its lack of proper reinvestment into the business. As a result its earnings growth has also been quite disappointing. Having said that, looking at current analyst estimates, we found that the company's earnings growth rate is expected to see a huge improvement. data.title: Orora (ASX:ORA) Margin Lift To 5.7% Tests Valuation Narratives After One Off Loss
data.description:

Financial results set the stage for Orora (ASX:ORA)

Orora (ASX:ORA) has put fresh H1 2026 numbers on the table, with total revenue of A$1,061.9 million and basic EPS of A$0.050, setting a clear reference point after a period that also featured earnings from discontinued operations and one off items in prior periods. The company has seen revenue move from A$1,055.7 million in H2 2024 to A$1,028.3 million in H1 2025 and then A$1,061.9 million in H2 2025, while EPS shifted from A$0.054 to effectively flat at A$0.0001 before landing at A$0.050. This gives investors a recent history of how the top line and per share earnings have tracked into this latest print. With net profit margins for the last year higher than the prior year and a large one off loss now quantified, the current result gives the market a cleaner read on how the core business is translating revenue into profit.

Margins at 5.7% with one off loss in the mix

  • Over the last 12 months, Orora reported a 5.7% net profit margin compared with 3.3% a year earlier, but that period also includes a one off A$117.9 million loss that affects how cleanly you can read the profit line.
  • Analysts' consensus view expects margins to benefit from sustainability and efficiency themes. However, the presence of that A$117.9 million loss means:
    • The improved reported margin to 5.7% versus 3.3% sits alongside trailing earnings that still reflect that loss, so some of the margin story is about accounting timing rather than just business strength.
    • The consensus view that margins could rise further is being measured off a base that includes both higher reported margin and a large loss, so you need to separate one off effects from what the H1 2026 core EPS of A$0.050 is actually showing.

At the same time, the 21.6x trailing P/E already sits above industry and peers, so the market is not treating Orora as a deep value laggard.

    • Trailing earnings over the past five years declined at about 2.9% a year, so the 4.52% yield is sitting on a track record where profits have not consistently grown, which can make a high payout ratio harder to sustain.
    • With H1 2026 basic EPS at A$0.050 and trailing results containing that A$117.9 million one off loss, critics argue there is limited margin for error if earnings growth does not come through in line with the roughly 13% yearly forecasts.

data.title: Rexel (ENXTPA:RXL) Margin Compression To 1.3% Tests Bullish Electrification Narratives
data.description:

The company has seen revenue move from €9.6b in the first half of 2024 to €9.8b in the first half of 2025, while basic EPS shifted from €1.17 to €0.88 over the same periods. Margins Thin, Net Profit Margin Sits at 1.3%

  • Over the last 12 months, Rexel generated €19.4b in revenue and €591.4m in net income, which works out to a 1.3% net profit margin compared with 3.7% a year earlier.
  • Bulls point to Rexel's growing exposure to electrification and digital sales as a way to lift margins over time. However, the current 1.3% margin and the €291.8m one off loss show that, for now, profitability is still sensitive to shocks and weak European conditions.
    • Consensus narrative highlights digital channels at 34% of group sales and 43% in Europe, but the recent margin level suggests this extra efficiency has not fully offset cost pressure and softer demand.
    • The bullish view expects margins to move higher over several years, while the latest trailing figures remind you that any improvement is starting from a relatively low base.

EPS Volatility and One Off Loss Challenge the Bear Case

  • Basic EPS moved from €1.17 in first half 2024 to €0.88 in first half 2025, and trailing EPS was also hit by the €291.8m one off loss included in the last 12 months.
  • Bears worry that dependence on construction and digital investment could mean weaker cash generation. At the same time, the trailing €591.4m net income and the swing back from a €12.8m loss in second half 2024 to €260.4m profit in first half 2025 show the business is still producing earnings despite that large charge.
    • Cautious narratives highlight risks from automation and direct purchasing, but the data center and broadband exposure in North America mentioned by bulls sits alongside these risks rather than being reflected in collapsing profits.
    • The one off loss clearly hurt trailing EPS, yet it is described as a single event, which is different from a steady structural erosion that some bearish arguments focus on.
Bears watching the one off loss and margin compression may want to see how those concerns stack up against detailed downside assumptions and longer term EPS targets. 🐻 Rexel Bear Case

Rich Valuation vs €27.44 DCF Fair Value

  • Rexel's trailing P/E is 44.6x against peer and European trade distributor averages of 30.6x and 17.9x, while the DCF fair value of €27.44 sits well below the current €37.67 share price.
  • Supporters of the bullish view lean on forecast earnings growth of about 19.5% a year and margin expansion from the current 1.3% level. The higher than average P/E and gap to DCF fair value underline how much those forecasts need to deliver to justify today's pricing.
    • The dividend yield of 3.19% with weak earnings coverage and high debt in the data means part of the bull story has to be about future profit growth rather than current payout strength.
    • Consensus and bullish narratives both anticipate earnings reaching several hundred million euros higher by 2028, while the present P/E and DCF fair value figures show that the market is already factoring in a significant improvement.

data.title: Ferrari (BIT:RACE) Margins Hold Above 22% Challenging Bullish Expansion Narratives
data.description:
Ferrari (BIT:RACE) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of about €1.8 billion and basic EPS of €2.14, rounding out a trailing twelve month picture that includes €7.1 billion in revenue and EPS of €8.97. Over recent quarters, the company has seen revenue move from €1.64 billion in Q3 2024 to €1.74 billion in Q4 2024 and then to €1.80 billion in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS has tracked in a tight range between €2.08 and €2.38. With trailing net profit margins holding in the low 20s, this set of results keeps the focus firmly on the durability of Ferrari’s profitability profile from here.

See our full analysis for Ferrari.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings run rate lines up with the key narratives around Ferrari’s growth, pricing power and margin resilience, and where those stories might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Ferrari

Advertisement

4.9% Earnings Growth Lags 18.3% Trend

  • Over the last twelve months, earnings grew 4.9% and trailing EPS reached €8.97, compared with a five year average earnings growth rate of 18.3% per year.
  • Consensus narrative sees new electric and hybrid models plus personalization as drivers of resilient long term earnings, yet the gap between the recent 4.9% earnings growth and the 18.3% five year pace raises questions about how quickly these drivers are flowing through to the bottom line.
    • Revenue on a trailing basis is €7.1b compared with €6.5b a year earlier, which lines up with the idea of a broader product and customization push supporting the top line.
    • At the same time, quarterly EPS in FY 2025 sat in a tight band of roughly €2.08 to €2.38, which suggests the earnings step up that bullish and consensus narratives anticipate has not yet moved materially beyond the current run rate.

Margins Hold Above 22% With Slight Compression

  • Net profit margin on the trailing twelve months is 22.3%, a touch below the prior year level of 22.8%, while FY 2025 quarterly net income ranged from about €380 million to €424 million on roughly €1.8b of revenue per quarter.
  • Bulls argue that expanded hybrid and electric offerings plus high margin personalization can support margin expansion, yet the small step down from 22.8% to 22.3% shows that, so far, margins are holding at a high level rather than clearly moving higher.
    • The bullish narrative talks about profit margins potentially rising toward 24.0%, but the current trailing 22.3% margin suggests any future improvement would need to build from an already strong, but slightly compressed, base.
    • Quarterly net income in FY 2025 stayed close to €400 million even as revenue fluctuated between about €1.77b and €1.80b, which fits the bullish view of disciplined operations, but does not yet show a decisive shift toward the higher margin profile bulls describe.
On these numbers, bulls say Ferrari’s high margins are the starting point for the next leg of growth, not the ceiling, and they point to electrification and personalization as the key reasons why. 🐂 Ferrari Bull Case

Premium P/E And DCF Gap Stand Out

  • Ferrari trades on a P/E of 36.4x against a global auto industry average of 20.3x and a peer average of 19.1x, while the current share price of €328.8 sits far above the DCF fair value estimate of €102.25.
  • Bears highlight that this valuation premium already bakes in strong growth, and the combination of a 36.4x P/E with 4.9% earnings growth and a DCF fair value well below the share price gives them numerical support for that concern.
    • The DCF gap, with the share price more than triple the €102.25 DCF fair value, points to the market paying a large multiple of the modelled cash flows, which is exactly the kind of stretch bearish investors flag.
    • Analysts’ medium term earnings growth expectation of roughly 7.1% per year is higher than the latest 4.9% outcome, so any stumble against those expectations could matter more when the starting valuation is already well above sector and peer P/E levels.
Skeptics often focus on how much optimism is already embedded in the price, and this earnings snapshot gives them plenty of hard numbers to point to. 🐻 Ferrari Bear Case

data.description:
• Vertex Pharmaceuticals (NasdaqGS:VRTX) has entered an exclusive license and collaboration with WuXi Biologics to develop a trispecific T-cell Engager for B-cell mediated autoimmune diseases.
  • The agreement focuses on a novel immunotherapy approach that aims to redirect T cells toward B cells involved in autoimmune activity.
  • The partnership expands Vertex's pipeline beyond cystic fibrosis and gene editing into autoimmune indications that can represent large treatment markets.

For you as an investor, this move broadens what has been a relatively concentrated focus on cystic fibrosis and gene editing into a new immunology area. Autoimmune diseases attract significant R&D attention, and trispecific T-cell Engagers are part of a newer class of therapies that many large biopharma names are exploring.

This alliance could influence how you think about Vertex's future mix of assets, risk profile, and potential revenue sources over time. The key questions to watch from here are how quickly the program moves through early development, what indications Vertex prioritizes, and whether the WuXi Biologics platform helps Vertex build a repeatable presence in autoimmune drug development.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Vertex Pharmaceuticals by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Vertex Pharmaceuticals.

📰 Beyond the headline: 0 risks and 3 things going right for Vertex Pharmaceuticals that every investor should see.

This alliance fits into Vertex’s push to broaden beyond cystic fibrosis by adding a new autoimmune pillar that could eventually sit alongside kidney disease, pain and gene-editing programs. WuXi Biologics is supplying the trispecific T-cell Engager design and research services, while Vertex takes on global development and commercialization rights. For you, the key takeaway is that Vertex is using its cystic fibrosis cash flows to access external science in an area where peers such as AbbVie, Johnson & Johnson and Amgen are also active, rather than building everything in house. The structure of upfront, milestone and royalty payments to WuXi means Vertex’s financial exposure can scale with progress instead of being concentrated at the preclinical stage.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Vertex Pharmaceuticals Narrative

  • This deal is consistent with the narrative that Vertex is using its cystic fibrosis franchise to fund a broader portfolio in autoimmune and precision medicine, adding another potential growth driver alongside povetacicept, zimislecel and other late stage assets.
  • At the same time, more early stage programs can add to R&D spending and execution complexity, which ties back to concerns in the narrative that diversification could weigh on margins if new areas are slower to scale than cystic fibrosis.
  • The trispecific T-cell Engager approach in B-cell-mediated autoimmune disease is not fully reflected in the existing narrative focus on pain, kidney disease and gene editing, so this alliance may represent an incremental source of optionality.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Vertex Pharmaceuticals to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Preclinical autoimmune programs carry a high risk of clinical setbacks or delays, so this trispecific T-cell Engager may take years before it contributes meaningfully, if at all.
  • ⚠️ Additional alliances can increase reliance on external partners and create future royalty and milestone obligations that may weigh on the profitability of any successful product.
  • 🎁 The deal expands Vertex’s opportunity set in autoimmune diseases, potentially adding a new driver beside cystic fibrosis and late stage kidney and pain assets if development progresses well.
  • 🎁 Access to WuXi Biologics’ T-cell Engager platform and research services may help Vertex build a broader autoimmune portfolio without having to build all of the discovery capabilities internally.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how quickly Vertex and WuXi advance the trispecific T-cell Engager through preclinical work and into first-in-human studies, along with any disclosure on initial target indications. Investor updates around R&D spend, especially at upcoming earnings where revenue growth from cystic fibrosis, Casgevy and Journavx is in focus, will show how this alliance fits into the broader capital allocation plan. You may also want to track how Vertex positions this autoimmune program versus competitors working on T-cell Engagers and B-cell-targeting therapies, as that will influence how differentiated the opportunity could be.

data.title: Is Cognex (CGNX) Fairly Priced After Recent Share Price Recovery?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Cognex is fairly priced or if there is still value left on the table, this article is designed to walk you through that question in a clear, practical way.
  • Cognex shares last closed at US$43.03, with returns of 5.6% over 7 days, 9.1% over 30 days, 16.5% year to date and 10.6% over 1 year, set against longer term returns of a 21.9% decline over 3 years and a 49.1% decline over 5 years that some investors may still have in mind.
  • Recent coverage around Cognex has focused on its role in industrial automation and machine vision applications, as investors weigh how this exposure fits into their long term themes. This backdrop helps frame the recent price moves as the market reassesses how much it is willing to pay for that exposure over time.
  • Simply Wall St currently assigns Cognex a valuation score of 1 out of 6, meaning it screens as undervalued on 1 of 6 checks. Next, we will look at what traditional valuation methods say about the stock before finishing with a broader way to think about its value.

Cognex scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Cognex Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s terms.

For Cognex, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity framework, starting from last twelve months free cash flow of about US$204.8 million. Analysts have supplied several years of projections, with Simply Wall St extending these further out. For example, projected free cash flow in 2029 is US$350 million, and by 2035 the extrapolated figure used in the model is US$524.5 million, all in US$ terms.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about US$43.58 per share. Against the recent share price of US$43.03, this implies a modest 1.3% discount, which is very small.

On this DCF view, Cognex appears broadly in line with its estimated cash flow value rather than clearly cheap or expensive.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Cognex is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Cognex.

Approach 2: Cognex Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Cognex, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. Investors usually accept a higher P/E when they expect stronger growth or see the earnings as relatively resilient, and a lower P/E when they see more risk or more cyclical profits.

Cognex currently trades on a P/E of 65.47x. That compares with an Electronic industry average P/E of 27.08x and a peer group average of 53.41x, so the shares trade at a higher multiple than both of those benchmarks.

Simply Wall St also provides a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for Cognex of 33.84x. This is an estimate of what a reasonable P/E might be, given the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, size, risk characteristics and industry. Because it blends these company specific factors rather than just lining Cognex up against broad industry or peer averages, the Fair Ratio is intended to be a more tailored reference point.

Comparing the current P/E of 65.47x with the Fair Ratio of 33.84x suggests the shares are pricing in more than the model implies is reasonable.

Result: OVERVALUED

They link those forecasts to a Fair Value, and then compare that Fair Value with today’s price using easy tools on Simply Wall St’s Community page, where millions of investors share Narratives that update automatically when fresh news or earnings arrive. For example, you might see one Cognex Narrative that lines up with the more cautious US$35 fair value and another that leans toward the more optimistic US$58 fair value. By choosing the story that best fits your own expectations, you get a clear, consistent framework for deciding whether the current price of around US$43 makes sense for you.

For Cognex, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Cognex Narratives:

Each one ties a clear story to specific numbers, so you can decide which version of the future feels closer to your own view and then judge the current price of US$43.03 against that.

🐂 Cognex Bull Case

data.title: BAWAG Group (WBAG:BG) Margin Compression Tests Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
BAWAG Group (WBAG:BG) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €511.6 million and basic EPS of €2.99, capping off a trailing twelve month run that produced €1.99 billion in revenue and EPS of €11.17. The bank has seen revenue move from €1.51 billion and EPS of €9.40 over the trailing period ending Q4 2024 to €1.99 billion and EPS of €11.17 by Q4 2025, with net income over the same window rising from €738.0 million to €859.9 million. With a current share price of €133.50, these results highlight revenue and earnings trends that appear solid, and investors may watch closely to see how margins and profitability metrics evolve from here.

See our full analysis for BAWAG Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the widely held narratives around BAWAG Group, and where the latest data supports or challenges those stories.

See what the community is saying about BAWAG Group

Advertisement

Margins Strong, But Off Last Year’s Peak

  • Over the last 12 months, BAWAG generated €859.9 million of net income on €1.99b of revenue, which works out to a 43.2% net profit margin, compared with 48.7% a year earlier in the dataset.
  • Consensus narrative talks about efficiency gains from integrating Knab and Barclays Consumer Bank Europe, yet the data shows margin pressure over the trailing period, with:
    • Quarterly cost to income ratios in 2025 sitting around the mid 30s, from 37% in Q1 to 36.1% in Q3, alongside rising quarterly revenue from €474.6 million to €511.6 million.
    • EPS over the last 12 months at €11.17 versus €9.40 a year earlier, so higher profitability per share is sitting next to a slightly lower margin, which partly challenges the idea that efficiency alone is driving the story.

Loan Quality And 71% Bad-Loan Cover

  • Non performing loans over the trailing period sit in the €601 million to €666 million range, while the allowance for bad loans is reported at 71%, indicating provisions do not fully cover that non performing loan base.
  • Bears argue that concentration in DACH and NL markets and tight regulation could make credit costs more important, and the current numbers give that concern some backing, with:
    • Non performing loans at €664 million on a trailing 12 month basis in Q3 2025, alongside total loans of about €49.7b in earlier quarters, so problem loans are a visible, if not dominant, slice of the book.
    • An unstable dividend record in the analysis summary paired with this 71% coverage level, which together reinforce the cautious view that capital allocation and provisioning are areas to monitor rather than take for granted.
On this mix of healthy earnings and only partially covered bad loans, skeptics see plenty to question in how durable the current profitability really is. 🐻 BAWAG Group Bear Case

P/E Of 11.9x And Big Gap To DCF Fair Value

  • With the share price at €133.50 and trailing EPS at €11.17, BAWAG trades on a P/E of 11.9x, which the data shows as below the peer average of 13.7x and slightly above the European banks average of 11.3x, while the DCF fair value cited is €273.87, far above the current price.
  • Bulls point to this valuation setup as a key part of their case, and the current figures give them some clear talking points, including:
    • Trailing EPS growth of 16.5% over the last year alongside that 11.9x P/E, which is framed as cheaper than peers in the analysis, suggesting the market is not paying a premium for the recent growth shown in the dataset.
    • The DCF fair value of €273.87 versus the €133.50 share price, which is described as about a 51.3% gap, aligning with bullish arguments that current pricing leaves a wide margin between trading levels and the long term cash flow estimate.
If you want to see how optimistic investors connect these numbers to their thesis, it is worth reading the full bullish case they are using. 🐂 BAWAG Group Bull Case

data.title: JM Group (HKEX:JMG) Earnings Growth Driven By Non Cash Margin Raises Questions For Bulls
data.description:
JM Group (JMG) has put fresh numbers on the table for FY 2025, with second half revenue of HK$122.0 million and basic EPS of HK$0.39, while the trailing twelve months show revenue of HK$269.4 million and EPS of HK$1.17 backed by net income of HK$18.7 million. The company has seen revenue move from HK$92.1 million and EPS of HK$0.12 in 2H 2024 to HK$147.3 million and EPS of HK$0.78 in 1H 2025, before landing at the latest half year figures. Over the past year, earnings growth of 165.4% has taken net margin to 6.9% versus 3.2% a year earlier. For investors, that mix of higher reported profitability and a bigger non cash component in earnings sets up a results season where margin quality matters as much as margin level.

See our full analysis for JM Group.

With the headline numbers on the screen, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the prevailing market and community narratives around JM Group, and where those stories might need updating.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

165.4% Earnings Growth, But 6.9% Margin Relies Heavily On Non Cash Items

  • Over the last 12 months JM Group reports earnings growth of 165.4% and a net profit margin of 6.9%, compared with 3.2% a year earlier, alongside trailing twelve month net income of HK$18.7 million on HK$269.4 million of revenue.
  • What stands out for a bullish view that likes the higher margin is that a high portion of those earnings is non cash, which means:
    • The 6.9% margin and HK$18.7 million of net income look stronger on paper than they might in pure cash terms, so bulls need to check how much of that is backed by actual cash generation.
    • The 165.4% earnings growth lines up with the margin move from 3.2% to 6.9%, but the emphasis on non cash items means that investors who focus on cash backed profits may treat that growth more cautiously than the headline suggests.

Halves Swing From HK$1.9m To HK$12.4m To HK$6.2m Profit

  • The last three half year periods show net income moving from HK$1.9 million in 2H 2024 to HK$12.4 million in 1H 2025, then HK$6.2 million in 2H 2025, alongside revenue of HK$92.1 million, HK$147.3 million and HK$122.0 million respectively.
  • Critics who take a more bearish stance on the stability of the business focus on how uneven that profit line is, and the numbers give them some support:
    • The jump from HK$1.9 million to HK$12.4 million and then to HK$6.2 million shows that profit does not move in a straight line with revenue, which matters in a wholesale style model where margins can be thin.
    • When you set those half year figures against the trailing twelve month net income of HK$18.7 million, bears can point out that a big share of the trailing result is concentrated in one strong half, rather than being evenly spread across periods.
Over these swings in half year profit, many investors want to understand how the story could evolve beyond a single reporting period, and 📊 Read the full JM Group Consensus Narrative.

P/E Of 56.3x And Shares At HK$6.61 Against DCF Fair Value Of HK$24.33

  • Supporters who lean bullish on valuation point to the gap between HK$6.61 and the DCF fair value of HK$24.33, but the detailed data add some important context:
    • The P/E of 56.3x is higher than the 17.3x industry average, so even with the discount to DCF fair value the shares are not cheap on every yardstick when you compare them to the broader industry.
    • At the same time, risks such as high debt, highly illiquid shares and reported negative shareholders’ equity sit alongside that valuation gap, which helps explain why the market price may sit well below the DCF fair value even after a 165.4% earnings lift.

data.title: Is Celanese (CE) Using Dividend And Price Hikes To Quietly Recast Its Margin Strategy?
data.description:
• Celanese Corporation recently declared a quarterly cash dividend of US$0.03 per share on its common stock, payable on March 10, 2026 to shareholders of record as of February 24, 2026, and earlier this week raised prices for acetic acid, vinyl acetate monomer and related derivatives in the Western Hemisphere.
  • This combination of a modest cash return to shareholders and immediate pricing actions in key chemical products underscores management’s focus on margin protection amid cautious earnings expectations.
  • Next, we’ll examine how Celanese’s recent acetic acid and vinyl acetate monomer price increases may influence its existing investment narrative.

Celanese Investment Narrative Recap

To own Celanese, you need to believe that its acetyl and engineered materials portfolio can translate into resilient cash generation despite recent losses and soft demand. The latest US$0.03 dividend and Western Hemisphere price hikes in acetic acid and vinyl acetate monomer look modest against the near term earnings risk highlighted by bearish estimates, and do not materially change the key catalyst of a cleaner earnings print and clearer volume recovery path.

The most relevant announcement here is the immediate price increases in acetic acid and vinyl acetate monomer, given consensus concerns about overcapacity and margin pressure in these chains. How much of these hikes actually sticks in the face of weak order visibility and customer destocking will be important for assessing whether Celanese’s margin profile can stabilize ahead of the 17 February earnings update.
